This would bring the total return on the idea to ~50% from my initial coverage, not what I hoped, but that's why you buy with a margin of safety! Disagree with my estimates? Let me know why in the comments.

data.title: SHLD: Modern Warfare, Modern Returns
data.description:
The Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD) is a paradigm change in the defense industry, which breaks through traditional heavy industry to focus on the high-margin realm of software-defined warfare. Its portfolio focuses on a niche where intelligence meets impact by identifying the foundational elements of contemporary national security: cybersecurity, robotics, and autonomous drones. In addition to the brains of warfare, such as advanced processors, sensor arrays, and data networking solutions, SHLD also allows investors to tap into the critical physical platforms, such as military aircraft, naval platforms, and advanced weapon systems. Rather than simply focusing on niche elements such as space launch platforms, defense-oriented fuel solutions, and technical simulation-based training solutions, SHLD allows investors to tap into the entire defense value chain.

The fund is entering 2026 on the back of a stellar performance in 2025 as the fund rose by an astonishing 73%. The performance was significantly robust compared to the broader market. However, the run has yet to end, as the fundamentals from several powerful catalysts will continue to support the fund. Total defense spending is on course to top $3.6 trillion by the end of the decade due to a massive rearmament cycle underway. Let's take a look at the fund, the catalysts, the various risks, the valuation, and the conclusion.

The Fund

Global X Defense Tech ETF has a main goal of investing in companies at the forefront of defense technology. The companies span from cybersecurity companies and advanced military systems builders to hardware companies operating in the space of robotics, fuel systems, and aircraft defense applications. The fund is set to replicate the performance of the Global X Defense Tech Index. You can read up in detail regarding the index on the link; I will cover some of the more important facts. The index consists of companies in three sub-themes: Cybersecurity, Defense Technology, and Advanced Military Systems. Only companies with revenues above 50% earned in these subthemes are included in the index. The index consists of 50 stocks. The criteria of the selection pool are relatively strict, in my opinion, and provide stakeholders with a generally higher-quality pool of companies. The companies must be listed in the developed or Emerging Markets range, excluding China, India, Kuwait, Pakistan, Russia, and Saudi Arabia. Market cap must be at least $200 million for new constituents and at least $160 million for already included companies. Average daily traded value in the last 6 months has to be a minimum of $2 million for pre-selection, must be traded for at least 90% of the trading days for the last 6 months, and free float must be at least 10% of all outstanding shares. The cap is set at 8% for a single company, and all companies with weights above 5% cannot exceed 40% of the index; all the other companies are capped at 4.5%. These criteria alone prohibit the fund from being overly concentrated.

We will rely on Seeking Alpha’s grading system to do an overview of the fund characteristics and compare it to peer funds. The fund has a Seeking Alpha C+ Expense Grade, with a 0.5% expense ratio to blame for the grade; however, I would say that the expense ratio is comparable to other thematic funds. The Bid/Ask % Spread is alone graded A-, with a 0.04% Bid/Ask % Spread. Regarding liquidity, the fund has a Seeking Alpha A Grade, with ample daily liquidity, an average of 1.19 million shares traded daily over the last three months, and the average volume being around $82 million. Assets under management are also at a substantial level, being around $7 billion. The dividend yield is 0.46%, while the dividend growth rate has been high over the last two years. This earns the fund a Dividend Grade C.

The sector allocation is Industrials-heavy, with around 87% of fund holdings. Technology comes next with around 13%.

The fund has diversified international exposure while having a dominating United States exposure with around 60% of fund assets. Germany, Britain, and South Korea come next with 8.3%, 6.5%, and 5.9%, respectively. We know that defense cannot be outsourced, and the exposure to companies of these economies provides the fund the potential to benefit from the ongoing rearmament of Europe and wider geopolitics.

Compared to peers in the industry, SHLD is lagging in categories such as Expenses and Dividends, behind peer funds State Street® SPDR® S&P® Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR) and iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA). I do not think this is a handicap for the fund, as the prime investment thesis behind it is that the companies are growth companies, poised to benefit from structural trends, and the fund should not be looked at as a dividend fund, and we also covered that the expense ratio is elevated, but not so much for a thematic fund. The fund is ranked best in the Momentum, Risk, and Liquidity criteria.

SHLD has been operating since the end of 2023, and we will do a comparison with peer funds having that in mind.

The fund has delivered fantastic returns, with around 208% cumulatively for the period, far higher than the second-best performer, XAR, with around 146%.

$3.6 Trillion Defense Supercycle Underway

The investment thesis supporting the Global X Defense Tech ETF, begins with the potential U.S. 2027 Department of Defense budget, which would mean an increase in spending by 50% to $1.5 trillion and signaling the most radical departure ever from traditional spending habits, with much of the allocation potentially going to initiatives such as Sovereign AI, and the Golden Dome, multi-layered missile shield which is expected to cost around $500 billion and would embroil potential contractors Lockheed Martin(LMT), RTX(RTX) and L3Harris Technologies(LHX), all constituents of the fund. Furthermore, these technologies are further tested in real-time in real-world situations such as Operation Absolute Resolve in Venezuela, furthering their proof of operational strength and proof of concept validity for autonomous drones and electronic warfare suites, potentially triggering new orders for constituents such as L3Harris.

As such, this operational success is supercharging a broader global rearmament cycle where projected global defense spending now is on track to hit $3.6 trillion by 2030-nearly 33% above 2024 levels. Central to this growth is the European defense supercycle, wherein NATO nations have confirmed new 3.5% of GDP spending targets, bolstered by the EU's SAFE (Security Action for Europe) fund. This underpins a resilient revenue floor for SHLD's European champions, including Rheinmetall and Thales, as they modernize regional defenses. Most importantly, though, this transition toward software-defined systems is fundamentally changing the sector's financial profile, as companies move away from hardware-heavy production, and margin profiles are set to expand from historical low double digits toward levels consistent with the technology sector.

Looking forward towards the coming decade, the fund stands out as the main beneficiary of the Agentic AI and JADC2 regime. The Joint All-Domain Command and Control (JADC2) construct has an estimated market size of $56.9 billion by 2035, which essentially means creating a "network of networks," thereby enabling every sensor to communicate directly and instantly with every shooter. The JADC2 evolution has also been further spurred by Agentic AI, with the potential to augment as much as 36% of tasks performed in the industry, furthering the prospects of margin expansion. All in all, the wise exposure criteria of the fund have successfully shifted the fund to incline to higher-margin companies and encapsulated the global security landscape, which could be heading towards multi-decade growth, given geopolitical risks and the increasing polarization.

Valuation

After such a fantastic year for the fund, I expected to be met with adjusted, higher multiples, but quickly I realized that earnings far outpaced the price return and that P/E actually contracted, from an average 36.67x in 2025 to around 30x now.

Other multiples such as P/B and P/S are somewhat higher than the peer group, while long-term earnings are double-digit, above the average. Not a surprise here, given what we previously discussed.

The fund is in an upward trend, breaking above the 20-, 50-, and 200-day MA.

Risks

Although the structural pillars for the Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD) appear quite strong, the thesis faces a tricky risk stack for 2026, particularly around a potential re-rating given a parabolic 2025 performance. In other words, the P/E of approximately 30x and the current RSI of around 40 could mean potential weakness, given a disappointing quarter for the defense tech group could create a space for the fund to re-rate downwards. Another risk, a peace treaty between Ukraine and Russia, could end in 2026, with now the first trilateral talks between the US, Russia, and Ukraine taking place. We know the sensitivity of the sector to such activities, as witnessed by the temporary 4.5% drop in the sector after the 28-point Ukraine Peace Plan was unveiled by the US administration in November of last year. Politics is also a huge factor and will play a very important role. For one, the current administration is at risk of losing their Congress support during the 2026 midterm elections; thus, the huge $1.5 trillion budget could possibly be blocked or reduced considerably. Finally, the high-tech chip and defense industry is heavily reliant on rare earth minerals from China, and this creates potential risks connected to supply chain dependencies given any further trade escalations.

Conclusion

SHLD represents a defense tech-focused fund, underweighting the heavy industry low-margin traditional companies, and exposing itself to tech giants of modern war. After a whopping 73% increase last year, catalysts remain, such as an increasing U.S. defense budget, which might amount to $1.5 trillion in 2027, and the world that's on track to spend $3.6 trillion by 2030. Not to mention real-world successes in Venezuela proving this tech is effective. However, we should be mindful of the warning signs or risks to the BUY thesis: the Ukraine war ending and slowing down orders, the 2026 midterm elections making a split Congress block the budget or heavily chop it, and the continued reliance on China for the minerals needed to build high-tech chips. It’s an adequate long-term bet on the high-tech era of warfare, in my opinion, but expect heightened volatility given the risks we discussed.
